section_text_parts: The same way you can specify a response model, you can also declare the HTTP status code used for the response with the parameter status_code in any of the path operations :
- @app.get()
- @app.post()
- @app.put()
- @app.delete()
- etc.
The status_code parameter receives a number with the HTTP status code.
It will:
- Return that status code in the response.
- Document it as such in the OpenAPI schema (and so, in the user interfaces):
roots.section_text:
The same way you can specify a response model, you can also declare the HTTP status code used for the response with the parameter status_code in any of the path operations :

- @app.get()

- @app.post()

- @app.put()

- @app.delete()

- etc.

The status_code parameter receives a number with the HTTP status code.

It will:

- Return that status code in the response.

- Document it as such in the OpenAPI schema (and so, in the user interfaces):

section_text_parts: In HTTP, you send a numeric status code of 3 digits as part of the response.
These status codes have a name associated to recognize them, but the important part is the number.
In short:
- 100 - 199 are for "Information". You rarely use them directly.  Responses with these status codes cannot have a body.
- 200 is the default status code, which means everything was "OK".
- Another example would be 201 , "Created". It is commonly used after creating a new record in the database.
- A special case is 204 , "No Content".  This response is used when there is no content to return to the client, and so the response must not have a body.
- An example is 404 , for a "Not Found" response.
- For generic errors from the client, you can just use 400 .
children.section_text:
In HTTP, you send a numeric status code of 3 digits as part of the response.

These status codes have a name associated to recognize them, but the important part is the number.

In short:

- 100 - 199 are for "Information". You rarely use them directly.  Responses with these status codes cannot have a body.

- 200 is the default status code, which means everything was "OK".

- Another example would be 201 , "Created". It is commonly used after creating a new record in the database.

- A special case is 204 , "No Content".  This response is used when there is no content to return to the client, and so the response must not have a body.

- An example is 404 , for a "Not Found" response.

- For generic errors from the client, you can just use 400 .

section_text_parts: Let's see the previous example again:
201 is the status code for "Created".
But you don't have to memorize what each of these codes mean.
You can use the convenience variables from fastapi.status .
They are just a convenience, they hold the same number, but that way you can use the editor's autocomplete to find them:

Let's see the previous example again:

But you don't have to memorize what each of these codes mean.

You can use the convenience variables from fastapi.status .

They are just a convenience, they hold the same number, but that way you can use the editor's autocomplete to find them:

section_text_parts: Later, in the Advanced User Guide , you will see how to return a different status code than the default you are declaring here.

flat_nodes.section_text:
The same way you can specify a response model, you can also declare the HTTP status code used for the response with the parameter status_code in any of the path operations :

- @app.get()

- @app.post()

- @app.put()

- @app.delete()

- etc.

The status_code parameter receives a number with the HTTP status code.

It will:

- Return that status code in the response.

- Document it as such in the OpenAPI schema (and so, in the user interfaces):

In HTTP, you send a numeric status code of 3 digits as part of the response.

These status codes have a name associated to recognize them, but the important part is the number.

In short:

- 100 - 199 are for "Information". You rarely use them directly.  Responses with these status codes cannot have a body.

- 200 is the default status code, which means everything was "OK".

- Another example would be 201 , "Created". It is commonly used after creating a new record in the database.

- A special case is 204 , "No Content".  This response is used when there is no content to return to the client, and so the response must not have a body.

- An example is 404 , for a "Not Found" response.

- For generic errors from the client, you can just use 400 .

Let's see the previous example again:

201 is the status code for "Created".

But you don't have to memorize what each of these codes mean.

You can use the convenience variables from fastapi.status .

They are just a convenience, they hold the same number, but that way you can use the editor's autocomplete to find them:
Later, in the Advanced User Guide , you will see how to return a different status code than the default you are declaring here.

tutorial/response-status-code::section-0.section_text:
The same way you can specify a response model, you can also declare the HTTP status code used for the response with the parameter status_code in any of the path operations :

- @app.get()

- @app.post()

- @app.put()

- @app.delete()

- etc.

The status_code parameter receives a number with the HTTP status code.

It will:

- Return that status code in the response.

- Document it as such in the OpenAPI schema (and so, in the user interfaces):

In HTTP, you send a numeric status code of 3 digits as part of the response.

These status codes have a name associated to recognize them, but the important part is the number.

In short:

- 100 - 199 are for "Information". You rarely use them directly.  Responses with these status codes cannot have a body.

- 200 is the default status code, which means everything was "OK".

- Another example would be 201 , "Created". It is commonly used after creating a new record in the database.

- A special case is 204 , "No Content".  This response is used when there is no content to return to the client, and so the response must not have a body.

- An example is 404 , for a "Not Found" response.

- For generic errors from the client, you can just use 400 .

Let's see the previous example again:

201 is the status code for "Created".

But you don't have to memorize what each of these codes mean.

You can use the convenience variables from fastapi.status .

They are just a convenience, they hold the same number, but that way you can use the editor's autocomplete to find them:
Later, in the Advanced User Guide , you will see how to return a different status code than the default you are declaring here.

section_text_parts: Notice that status_code is a parameter of the "decorator" method ( get , post , etc). Not of your path operation function , like all the parameters and body.
admonition_nodes.section_text: Notice that status_code is a parameter of the "decorator" method ( get , post , etc). Not of your path operation function , like all the parameters and body.

section_text_parts: status_code can alternatively also receive an IntEnum , such as Python's http.HTTPStatus .
admonition_nodes.section_text: status_code can alternatively also receive an IntEnum , such as Python's http.HTTPStatus .

section_text_parts: Some response codes (see the next section) indicate that the response does not have a body.
FastAPI knows this, and will produce OpenAPI docs that state there is no response body.
admonition_nodes.section_text:
Some response codes (see the next section) indicate that the response does not have a body.

FastAPI knows this, and will produce OpenAPI docs that state there is no response body.

section_text_parts: If you already know what HTTP status codes are, skip to the next section.
admonition_nodes.section_text: If you already know what HTTP status codes are, skip to the next section.

section_text_parts: To know more about each status code and which code is for what, check the MDN documentation about HTTP status codes .
admonition_nodes.section_text: To know more about each status code and which code is for what, check the MDN documentation about HTTP status codes .

section_links.text: MDN documentation about HTTP status codes

admonition_nodes.title: Technical Details
section_text_parts: You could also use from starlette import status .
FastAPI provides the same starlette.status as fastapi.status just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
